summary: I was 21 years old in 1941 when the Attack on Pearl Harbor happened on December 7th. Someone told me about the bombing, and it was a shocking moment. After the United States went to war, we experienced rationing and shortages of many things. The event marked a turning point in our lives during the 1940s.
cues: United States enters World War II

summary: Dewey Follett Bartlett was my husband, born March 28, 1919 in Marietta, Ohio. We met at a dance and were married on April 2, 1945, just before the war ended. He graduated from Princeton in 1942 with a degree in geological engineering, served as a Marine Corps dive bomber pilot trained by the Navy, and later helped run the family oil business in Tulsa. Dewey was a handsome and charming man who I admired deeply, and we raised three children together.

Marietta, Ohio is where Dewey Follett Bartlett was born in 1919. It’s also where his father, David A. Bartlett, was a banker who eventually got involved in the oil business. Our family roots trace back there before moving to Oklahoma. It was a significant place in Dewey’s early life and family history.

Dewey graduated from Princeton University in 1942 with a degree in geological engineering. His education there was important because it prepared him for his later work in the oil business. Princeton was a prestigious place and a proud achievement for him before he joined the Marine Corps during World War II.

Dewey served in the United States Marine Corps starting in 1942 during World War II. He was trained by the Navy as a dive bomber pilot. Although he didn’t talk much about his war experiences, I remember one story where he missed bombing a target but hit a railroad track instead. His service was a big part of his life before we married in 1945.

The Navy trained Dewey as a dive bomber pilot while he was serving in the Marine Corps during World War II. This training was crucial for his role in the war. Although Dewey didn’t share many stories, the Navy’s involvement shaped his military career.

Keener Oil Company was the family business that Dewey and his brother David took over after World War II. It was started by their father, who got oil leases in Titusville, Pennsylvania, as payment on a loan. Dewey and David worked summers as roustabouts and later ran the company in Tulsa until Dewey sold his share when he became a senator. The company was central to our family’s life and livelihood.

David Bartlett was Dewey’s brother who also served in the Navy. After the war, he and Dewey came to Tulsa to manage the Keener Oil Company together. David had an engineering degree from MIT, and the two brothers worked closely in the oil business. He was an important figure in Dewey’s life and our family’s oil ventures.

Titusville, Pennsylvania is where the oil was first discovered and where our family’s oil interests began. Dewey’s father received oil leases there as payment on a loan, which eventually led to drilling wells in Oklahoma. It was the starting point for the Keener Oil Company and our family’s involvement in the oil industry.

David Bartlett, Dewey’s brother, graduated from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology with an engineering degree. His education complemented Dewey’s geological engineering degree from Princeton and helped them run the family oil business. MIT was a key part of David’s background before they settled in Tulsa.

After World War II, Dewey and his brother David moved to Tulsa, Oklahoma to take over the family oil company. Tulsa became our home where we raised our children and where Dewey was involved in oil and ranching. We lived in several places around Tulsa, including near Peoria and later on 30th Street, before moving to the governor’s house and downtown. Tulsa was central to our family life and Dewey’s career.

Vandever’s was one of the downtown Tulsa department stores where we shopped in the 1950s. Along with Clark’s Good Clothes, Froug’s, and Brown-Duncan, it was part of the shopping scene where people dressed up and wore hats and gloves. Shopping downtown was a special experience and a memorable part of life back then.

Clark’s Good Clothes was another popular downtown Tulsa store where my husband used to buy his clothes. It was part of the cluster of stores like Vandever’s and Froug’s that made downtown shopping a refined experience. People really dressed up when they went there, which made it a special occasion.

Froug’s was one of the well-known department stores downtown Tulsa where we often shopped in the 1950s. It was part of the vibrant shopping district along with Vandever’s, Clark’s Good Clothes, and Brown-Duncan. These stores were all close together and made downtown Tulsa a lively place to visit.

Brown-Duncan was a big department store in downtown Tulsa during the 1950s. It was part of the main shopping area alongside Vandever’s, Clark’s Good Clothes, and Froug’s. Shopping there was a social event, and people dressed nicely when they went downtown.

Dewey took a Dale Carnegie speaking course during his recovery from hepatitis around 1959 or 1960. The class was taught by Eloise Worley, and they had a lot of fun with exercises like picking a subject out of a hat and giving impromptu speeches. Dewey loved the course and even won a pin for the best speech, which helped him build confidence in public speaking.
